feed.title: Proton’s Blog
feed.subtitle: A blog for CompProg, transit, and all things nerdy!

entry.title: Geoguessing Locations in a Train YouTuber’s Video

link.title: Geoguessing Locations in a Train YouTuber’s Video

entry.content:
Background Information on the Video

Before you continue with this blog, I recommend watching the video below.

The video above is from Tanner, my personal favorite transit YouTuber. He makes videos about interesting trains and places in Japan.

This particular video starts out like any other: Tanner records his train journey to an interesting spot. In this particular video, he takes the JR Tokaido line from Odawara (JT16) to Nebukawa (JT18).

Nebukawa station itself is a rather scenic station overlooking the ocean in Kanagawa prefecture. To my knowledge, Nebukawa—and, by extension, the Tokaido Main Line—is quite well-known among locals for this ocean view. It even appears in some J-pop songs, such as Orangestar’s “Noctiluca”.

「このまま海へ向かえ下れ東海道線
“I’m going out of town on the Tokaido line, heading towards the ocean”
- Orangestar (Translation by Shiru)

Tanner goes into more detail about Nebukawa in the video. The facts he gives in the video are rather intriguing, but what is more interesting—argurably—is the distinctive encounter that Tanner experiences in the video.

The Attack of the Pheasant

Figure 1: Tanner Gets Chased by a Pheasant

At around the time 9:53 in the video, Google Maps betrayed Tanner. It sent him to a small walkway. Little did he know, a Green Pheasant—the national bird of Japan—ferociously guarded these steps. As he approached, the pheasant resolved to chase Tanner away! The entire two-minute sequence became an inside joke—a meme of sorts—within Tanner’s community.

In fact, one year later, Tanner fans still make memes about this moment. Some fans even go as far as to make their channels pheasant-themed!

Figure 2: Comments Referencing the Pheasant Incident

Needless to say, this piece of Tanner history is perhaps one of the most important and well-known.

The Geodetective Work Begins

One day, I was rewatching the Nebukawa video. As a long-time viewer of Tanner’s channel (and one who has seen all of the videos1), I wished to know where exactly these places are on the map. After all, if I decide to go to Japan once more, I would like the option to experience firsthand the actual site of Tanner history.

Hence, I decided to put my Geoguessr skills to the test. And thus, for the next hour or so, I manifested my inner Rainbolt. Thankfully, I had an entire video to work with!

Finding the Exact Site of the Pheasant Incident

First things first: I set out to find the exact place where Tanner encountered the pheasant. I was oblivious to the fact that Tanner linked to this location in the description of the video, so I had to resort to some Geoguessr trickery to find it. (had I read the description, I would’ve saved 30 minutes 😅)

Figure 3: A Google Map of the Locality From Tanner’s Video

At around 9:44 in the video, we obtain an important clue: a Google Map of the local area of the site of the incident. The map gives us major clues of the location. I observed the following:

  1. There is a nearby spot called “Shion Work Place”.
  2. The dashed lines, which go from top to bottom in the map, represent the route of the Tokaido Shinkansen.

The first observation allowed me to find the general vicinity on Google Maps. The second observation allowed me to align the map correctly. These two observations together allowed me to narrow down the location. Behold:

This looks promising! But can we be sure this place is exactly where Tanner encountered the pheasant? In fact, we can. The following clip in the video shows the exact same sign that we can find in the street view above.

Figure 4: The Same Sign Found in the Video

And there we go! We have the exact spot of the Tanner pheasant incident!

At this point, I was getting rather excited. I proceeded to find the exact location of other spots in the video.

Finding the Exact Site of the Train Jumpscare

Figure 5: Tanner Gets Jumpscared by a Train

Later in the video, Tanner encounters a train crossing on his way to get dinner. By sheer coincidence, the warning bells in the crossing ring as he steps foot on it. The bird-traumatized Tanner was understantably startled by this incident. At the very least, he was greeted by a northbound JR freight train on the Tokaido Main Line tracks—a pleasant surprise for this train fan!

Knowing the exact location of the pheasant attack made this second part a lot easier. It turns out that the markings for this rail crossing are visible from satellite imagery!

Figure 6: Railroad Crossing Markings From Satellite Imagery

Unfortunately, I cannot link to the exact location on Google Maps. The location shown above is where you would end up if you continued past the railroad crossing.

After all of that, Tanner then unfortunately finds the restaurant he wants to go to closed (shown below). I found this restaurant by vicariously walking down the street in Google Earth.

The closure of this restaurant turned out to have a silver lining, however. As if to spite the birds, he opted to eat duck soba at the other nearby restaurant instead:

Figure 7: Tanner Eats Duck Soba

In the end, Tanner got even, and I got a list of potential places to visit if ever I do return to Japan!

Foreword

If you’d like to see the list of location pins, check this Google Earth project.

And if you haven’t already, go watch Tanner! He’s a relatively lowkey train YouTuber who goes to chill, unusual places in Japan. He uploads laidback content every week around Saturday!

The moral of the story: Never trust Google Maps! (joke)

  1. Big nerd, I know! ↩
entry.summary: Overanalyzing a video where a train YouTuber gets chased by a bird...

entry.title: Transit Treasures: Art Appreciation in a Fast-Paced Society

link.title: Transit Treasures: Art Appreciation in a Fast-Paced Society

entry.content:
Foreword

The following essay is my submission for the course CC0001: Inquiry and Communication in an Interdisciplinary World in Nanyang Technological University (NTU). I took this module as an opportunity to explore academic writing for humanities research (it’s also a requirement for undergraduates). I was blessed to have this essay nominated for publication for the Pioneer Road Journal. While the essay ultimately was not published, I improved a lot in choosing the right vocabulary and ensuring cohesion in my writing.

I opted to post the submission here to immortalize it. It would be a waste not to put it up somewhere after I had spent hours writing and refining it. I hope you find it an interesting read!

Transit Treasures: Art Appreciation in a Fast-Paced Society

Figure 1: The Crowd of Commuters at Dhoby Ghaut MRT During Rush Hour

A faint woosh hums through the platform. Two eyes of light emerge from the distance, accompanied by the crescendoing clatter of the Alstom R151’s wheels. The train screeches to a halt. The glass doors open. Commuters rush to the swiftly turning escalators like a turbulent stream. On the concourse, alighting passengers merge with hurried steps. A cacophony of footsteps reverberates against the granite floor. A card reader beeps. A fare gate swings open. A commuter rushes toward the closing train doors. His eyebrows furrow. A “tsk” of frustration escapes from his breath. I can feel the agony of waiting two minutes for the next train.

Figure 2: A Dhoby Washing Clothes Juxtaposed With Modern Landmarks

In contrast, still pieces of art bear witness to the fleeting movements in the surroundings: One depicting the rocks and leaves of the earth on towering gypsum columns, another illustrating a Dhoby surrounded by malls, hotels, and offices with the words “Dhoby Ghaut” in a cartoon art style. On the floor above, artists sit at pop-up booths, patiently waiting for hours on end for customers to pass by from nearby food stalls. They await the opportunity to share their art prints of imaginary road trips through vast cornfields. Despite the constant stream of workers passing by the art pieces and booths, the stream of footsteps ceases to halt. Not a single individual stops to look!

The unrelenting footsteps of the crowd and the frantic rush to beat closing doors both seem to indicate an acute awareness of the minute ebb and flow of time. Our present-day society enforces an artificial rhythm. Train schedules, office hours, and class timetables dictate our motions through space and time. We have become a society attuned to the swiftly turning hands of the clock. In a world of schedules and deadlines, of punctuality and productivity, our consciousness learned to discretise time to its smallest perceivable divisions.

The art installations featuring natural and historical elements seem to depict a slower time, far removed from the pace of present society. Artists seem content spending hours reflecting on their experiences, illustrating pieces to capture these, and waiting for curious passersby to stop and admire their works. Perhaps, in breaking free from the mechanical metronome of our fast-paced society, they realise authentic experiences—deep realities of humans, culture, history, and nature that cannot be gleaned from just a momentary look. This led me to wonder: How does the fast-paced nature of society influence how people view or value art?

In her master’s thesis, Guan (2023), an interior design graduate from the Pratt Institute, argues for the need to slow down from the fast pace of life due to work. She noted how art—particularly, museum art—enables us to slow down because it shows a slower pace of life in the past. In this way, art challenges us to reflect on our experiences and broader sociocultural values. This slow reflection creates what Guan calls a “relational experience” between an individual and an artwork, which “enables people to resonate with their surrounding world and explore individual emotion, human relations, and their social context” (Guan, 2023, pp. 6-7). Simply put, slow contemplation enables us to relate our lived experiences in terms of what the artwork depicts.

Art invites individuals to slow down and recontextualise their lived experiences in terms of the artwork’s cultural, historical, and humanist elements. It challenges us to explore the unseen, to step beyond our familiar world into the artist’s world. Guan (2023), however, does not address whether individuals today would initiate artistic contemplation in the first place. People nowadays seem not to be predisposed to contemplating historical art, as the apparent apathy of the commuters highlights.

On the surface, it seems that our fast-paced society inhibits such relational, contemplative experiences by reducing the time we have for contemplation. Daily worries, such as juggling leisure with work and family responsibilities, seem to inhibit contemplation. While plausible, this explanation fails to account for the sheer amount of time people engage with contemporary art. I, like many today, find myself spending hours deciphering the meaning of a film or disentangling the lyrics of a song. This behaviour is a stark contrast to the apparent lack of attention to the artworks in the MRT. What, then, imbues contemporary art forms with this appeal that historical art seems to lack?

A Channel News Asia (CNA) article by Sng (2025), a graduate of Communication Studies from Nanyang Technological University (NTU), highlights how art satisfies an individual’s desire to be remembered. Creative pursuits allow artists to unwind and reconnect with their emotions and values. Through this reconnection, individuals impress a part of themselves in their art. The art they create functions as a memento of their emotions and experiences. As Sng notes, “[writing poems] also helps me preserve my own personal history, and pushes me to keep pursuing new opportunities to write new chapters of my life story” (Sng, 2025, para. 31). Art records, enriches, and immortalises our lived experiences.

Sng (2025) reveals that art is our response to a deep-rooted desire to remember and be remembered. Art is a medium of recollection. Through it, we manifest our thoughts, feelings, and experiences into reality, enabling them to transcend the boundaries of time. I observed this in the pop-up stall owners in the MRT. Their willingness to wait for hours to sell their art perhaps signifies their desire to make their story heard and remembered. In turn, I found myself resonating with the stories told by this artist’s work. In contrast, I did not feel the same connection with the artworks in the MRT, finding them too unrelatable or abstract. Perhaps, then, the apparent apathy of the commuters stems from a lack of relatability with what the artworks depict.

Guan (2023) and Sng (2025) both agree that art functions as a tapestry of lived experiences. Individuals create art to immortalise their emotions and experiences—both past and present (Sng, 2025). Taken together, Guan and Sng reveal that art enables a perpetual cycle of unfolding dialogue through time. Past artists imbue art with their lived experiences (Sng, 2025). Art invites reflection on human existence and enriches future individuals’ lived experiences (Guan, 2023). Through the cycle of deliberate slow contemplation and creation, individuals continuously add to and enrich this tapestry of lived experiences over time.

Technological advancements made art accessible to a global audience. In our fast-paced society, many, like the artists in the pop-up stalls, turn to art as a means to slow down and record their feelings and experiences. The cross-temporal dialogue enabled by art remains seemingly prevalent today. However, the commuters’ attitude toward the artworks in the MRT leads me to question the integrity of this dialogue.

I initially conjectured that the lack of appreciation for the art in the MRT stemmed from a lack of time. The commuters moved like clockwork, a microcosm of the culture of efficiency Singaporeans find themselves in. In such a culture, perhaps individuals are too absorbed in everyday worries to appreciate art in their immediate environment. Now I realise that, beyond a lack of time, individuals gravitate toward contemporary and relatable art. Our fast-paced society has led us to value predictability, stability, and safety—including in the way we engage with art. We listen to love songs or watch films in our free time in part because we see in the characters, setting, or story a reflection of our lives. We seek familiarity and comfort in art. In contrast, the people and cultures depicted in historical art, like the painting of Dhobies in Dhoby Ghaut MRT, seem not to have struck a chord of relatability with the passersby. This should raise a concern—in neglecting historical art, we stand to lose a significant dimension of past human experiences and learnings.

Amidst our fast-paced society, the human desire to relate, create, and express remains. With these desires, art continues and will continue to be appreciated. Art, especially historical art, captures the richness and variety of human experiences across time. However, our fast-paced society has led us to seek the predictable and the familiar. In so doing, our world shrinks—we close ourselves off from the infinite depth and nuance of the human experience that such art depicts. Perhaps, then, the lack of appreciation of art was never the concern, but rather the lack of appreciation for the thoughts, feelings, ideas, and experiences expressed by past artists. We are at risk of forgetting what was. We are at risk of losing the chance to learn from the lives of those who lived before us. It is only when we willingly slow down and contemplate the unfamiliar that we can remember—that we can internalise—these past experiences to enrich our lives and those of others.

Acknowledgements

First, I would like to acknowledge my CC0001 professor for her advice on this essay. Thank you so much for patiently answering all of our questions after class!

Next, to the communication coaches in the LCC CommCube, thank you so much for your candid feedback! The discussions enabled me to clarify my ideas and write in a more focused and cohesive manner.

Further, I would like to thank my friend, Lauren, and my sister for proofreading the final submission to the Pioneer Road Journal. The advice you two gave with vocabulary usage and language conventions was the push I needed for the final submission.

I would like to thank my parents for the opportunity to study in NTU. NTU has not only facilitated my growth as a problem solver, but also taught me to think and live more independently. Your support has enabled this growth. This essay is one such manifestation of that growth.

And finally, I would like to thank God for all the blessings in the past year. That acceptance email sent to me on July 27th, 2025 was nothing short of a miracle. It was truly—and is truly—an insane canon event!

References

Guan, H. (2023). Slow space: reifying time to renew the interior as cultural register [Master’s thesis, Pratt Institute]. ProQuest Dissertations & Theses Global. https://ntu-sp.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/permalink/65NTU_INST/1j8pmes/cdi_proquest_journals_2864179390

Sng, E. (2025, July 19). Don’t have a creative hobby? Here’s why you should paint, sew or bake. Channel News Asia. https://www.channelnewsasia.com/today/mental-health-matters/creative-hobby-personal-leisure-arts-crafts-photography-mental-health-5239751
entry.summary: The following essay is my submission for the course CC0001: Inquiry and Communication in an Interdisciplinary World in Nanyang Technological University...

entry.title: The Great Mixing
entry.content:
Read the Problem Statement here

Solution

Basic Insights

Let’s start with a basic insight:

There are only 1001 unique coke types, corresponding to the concentrations \(\frac{0}{1000}, \frac{1}{1000}, \frac{2}{1000}, \cdots, \frac{1000}{1000}\). Therefore, we effectively have \(k \le 1001\).

Reinterpreting the Average

Concentration is defined as the volume of carbon dioxide divided by the total volume of coke. Therefore, we have the formula:

\[\dfrac{\sum_i a_i V_i}{\sum_i V_i} = n\]

Notice that the left-hand side is essentially a weighted average.

We want to minimize \(\sum_i V_i\) while satisfying the formula. Let’s eliminate the division operation by multiplying both sides by \(\sum_i V_i\):

\[\sum_i a_i V_i = n \sum_i V_i\] \[\sum_i a_i V_i = \sum_i nV_i\] \[\sum_i (a_i - n) V_i = 0\]

Let \(b_i = a_i - n\), then we have:

\[\sum_i b_i V_i = 0\]

Where \(-1000 \le b_i \le 1000\).

Thus, we have “reinterpreted the average”.

Now, we want to find an assignment of \(V_i\) such that \(\sum_i b_i V_i = 0\) and \(\sum_i V_i\) is minimized.

Reframing as a Shortest Paths Problem

The problem could be reframed as follows:

You are given an array of values \(B\) with elements \(b_0, b_1, b_2, \cdots b_{k - 1}\). You start with a sum \(s\) of \(0\). A move consists of setting \(s \leftarrow s + b_i\) for some \(b_i\). What is the minimum number of moves needed so that \(s\) is \(0\) once again?

The simplest solution would be to search through all possibilities: At each point, store all the reachable values after \(n\) moves.

We can model the space of all possibilities as a directed graph \(G\): Each node represents a possible sum, while each edge represents a possible move. Then, the optimal solution is the shortest cycle starting and ending at node \(0\).

We know how to find the shortest cycle in a directed graph: We simply use breadth-first search!

The time complexity of breadth-first search is \(O(\lvert V \rvert + \lvert E \rvert)\).

A Tiny Worry

One worry you may have is that the number of nodes and edges used is too large since we need to account for abritrarily large sums.

Thankfully, it can be shown that if an optimal solution exists, then we can rearrange the terms so that the partial sums are always in the interval \(I \in [-1000, 1000]\). This implies that we only need to consider the nodes \([-1000, 1000]\). The proof for this is shown in Appendix A.

Therefore, the time complexity of this solution is \(O(I^2 + K)\), where \(I\) is the magnitude of the denominator of the concentration values (in this case, \(I = 1000\)).

Reflection

What Didn’t Work

My first attempt at this problem involved reinterpreting the equation \(\sum_i b_i V_i = 0\) as the following vector equation:

\[\vec{b} \cdot \vec{V} = 0\]

Therefore, the problem reduces to finding a nonzero vector with nonnegative integer coordinates that’s orthogonal to \(\vec{b}\) and minimizes the sum of the coordinates.

There are many problems with this approach. For one, the subspace of vectors orthogonal to \(\vec{b}\) is, in general, a hyperplane. It’s difficult to conceptualize all of the lattice points that a hyperplane hits — if you go through the math, you’ll have to deal with a diophantine equation of \(K\) variables. Add the constraints that the solution must have nonnegative integer coordinates, and this becomes harder. Finally, the condition that the sum of the coordinates is minimized is awkward to work with under a linear algebra formulation.

In short, the idea of minimizing under all of these constraints is daunting. This suggested to me that a linear algebra solution would lead nowhere.

Motivation Behind the Solution

The solution that worked is essentially just complete search optimized with insights. Complete search could be reframed as searching through all possible states for an optimal solution. Then, complete search is simply a generalization of breadth-first search — the graph consists of states as nodes and transitions between states as edges.

Sometimes, it helps to think about brute force approaches. When you believe a brute force approach applies, it is useful to reframe the complete search as a breadth-first search over the graph of states. Then, making observations about the graph may lead to optimizations that make complete search tractable.

Finally, division is messy to work with. It may help to multiply both sides by a common factor to eliminate the need for division.

Appendices

Appendix A

Setup: Let \(s_i\) be the sequence of values in the array \(b_0, b_1, b_2, \cdots\) such that \(\sum_{i = 0}^{V^{*} - 1} s_i = 0\).

Note that \(V^{*}\) is the minimum volume needed to achieve a sum of zero.

Claim: There is some permutation of \(s_0, s_1, s_2, \cdots\), say \(s'_0, s'_1, s'_2, \cdots\), such that \(-1000 \le \sum_{i = 0}^{n} s'_i \le 1000\) for all \(0 \le n \le V^{*} - 1\).

Proof: The proof follows by construction.

Call the integers in the range \([-1000, 1000]\) as “good” and everything else as “bad”. We want all partial sums \(-1000 \le \sum_{i = 0}^{n} s'_i \le 1000\) to be good.

Let the current sum be \(k\). Initially, \(k = 0\).

If \(k > 0\), there is always an element \(s_i\) in \(S\) such that \(s_i < 0\). To see this, suppose, by contradiction, that all \(s_i\) in \(S\) is nonnegative and that \(k > 0\). Then, adding all of the elements in \(S\) to \(k\) results in a positive value, which is a contradiction since adding all elements to \(S\) must result in 0.

Mutatis mutandis, if \(k < 0\), there is always an element \(s_i\) in \(S\) such that \(s_i > 0\).

Now that we have the proper set-up, we can look at the construction.

We will keep adding elements \(s_i\) from the set \(S\) as follows:

When \(k = 0\), pick any element of \(S\) (say, \(s_i\)) then remove it from \(S\). Then, the new sum is \(k = s_i\), which is still good since \(s_i\) is good.

When \(k > 0\), pick an element of \(s_i\) that’s negative. note that since \(-1000 \le s_i < 0\) and \(0 < k \le 1000\), \(-1000 < s_i + k < 1000\), so the sum is still good.

When \(k < 0\), pick an element of \(s_i\) that’s positive. note that since \(0 < s_i \le 1000\) and \(-1000 \le k < 0\), \(-1000 < s_i + k < 1000\), so the sum is still good.

Since we have found a construction such that the running sums are all good, we have successfully proved the claim \(\blacksquare\).

entry.title: Monotonicity Induced by the Quadrangle Inequality

link.title: Monotonicity Induced by the Quadrangle Inequality

entry.content:
Intro

Consider a function \(f_d(x)\) and a cost function \(C(x,y)\). Here, \(f\) usually contains the DP state.

In general, we want to consider how to compute recurrences of the form:

\[f_{d + 1}(i) = \min_{0 \le j \lt i} f_{d} (j) + C(j, i)\]

Over the values \(f_{d + 1} (0), f_{d + 1}(1), f_{d + 1}(2), ..., f_{d + 1}(n - 1)\).

This is a common DP pattern in Competitive Programming. To see this, we will consider an example problem.

A Sample Problem

Read the problem statement here: https://atcoder.jp/contests/dp/tasks/dp_z

We will consider a variant of this problem: What is the minimum cost assuming you do exactly \(d\) hops?

Suppose that \(n = N\) and that \(1 \le n, d \le 5000\).

In short, the problem asks you to compute \(f_d (n)\), where \(f\) satisfies:

\[f_d (i) = \min_{0 \le j \lt i} f_{d - 1} (j) + C(j, i)\]

Here, \(C(j, i) = (h_i - h_j)^2\), and \(i \lt j \implies h_i \le h_j\).

Naive computation of this recurrence gives an \(O(dn^2)\) algorithm. Could we do better?

It turns out that we can do better! There is a certain property of \(C\) we can exploit that allows us to solve this problem in \(O(d n \log n)\). I won’t discuss it here for now, since it’s outside the scope of this blog, but if you’re curious, look up “Divide and Conquer DP”.

What is this property of \(C\), and why does it matter?

The Quadrangle Inequality

Suppose that \(L \le l \le r \le R\). Then, we say that \(C\) satisfies the Quadrangle Inequality iff:

\[C(L, r) + C(l, R) \le C(l, r) + C(L, R)\]

For example, when \(C(j, i) = (h_i - h_j)^2\), you can show that it satisfies the quadrangle inequality as follows. First, let’s start with the trivial inequalities \(h_l \ge h_L\) and \(h_R \ge h_r\). Thus, we have \(h_l - h_L \ge 0\) and \(h_R - h_r \ge 0\). Multiplying both identities together, we get:

\[\left(h_l - h_L\right) \left(h_R - h_r\right) \ge 0\]

Expanding…

\[h_L \left(h_r - h_R\right) + h_l \left(h_R - h_r\right) \ge 0\] \[h_L h_r + h_l h_R \ge h_l h_r + h_L h_R\] \[- 2h_L h_r - 2h_l h_R \le - 2h_l h_r - 2h_L h_R\] \[h_L^2 - 2h_L h_r + h_r^2 + h_l^2 - 2h_l h_R + h_R^2 \le h_l^2 - 2h_l h_r + h_r^2 + h_L^2 - 2h_L h_R + h_R^2\] \[\left(h_L - h_r\right)^2 + \left(h_l - h_R\right)^2 \le \left(h_l - h_r\right)^2 + \left(h_L - h_R\right)^2\]

Finally, substituting \(C(i, j) = \left(h_i, h_j\right)^2\),

\[C(L, r) + C(l, R) \le C(l, r) + C(L, R) \ \blacksquare\]

Thus, the cost function in Frog 3 satisfies the Quadrangle Inequality.

Sure, the cost function satisfies a special property, but why do we care about this property?

Monotonicity of Row Minima

It turns out that the value of \(j\) that minimizes \(f_d (i) = \min_{0 \le j \lt i} f_{d - 1}(j) + C(j, i)\) satisfies an interesting property when \(C\) satisfies the Quadrangle Inequality. (Try printing the values yourself for some concrete set of inputs and see if you can spot the pattern!)

Denote \(x_i\) as the value of \(j\) that minimizes \(f_{d - 1}(j) + C(j, i)\) for a fixed \(i\). Then, I claim that if \(k \lt k'\), then \(x_k \le x_{k'}\).

In other words, the row minima move to the right as you move down the rows. Since the minima continuously move to the right, we say that they are monotonically increasing.

Why does this phenomenon occur?

Proof of Monotonicity

Greedy insights can only take you so far—if left unproven, it may compromise the correctness of your algorithm. Therefore, we will prove that, if \(C\) satisfies the Quadrangle Inequality, we have \(x_k \le x_{k'}\).

Let’s simply denote \(f_d\) as \(f\)—We’re only going to consider a single value of \(d\) anyways. First, we know that \(j = x_k\) minimizes \(f(j) + C(j, k)\) for a fixed \(k\). Therefore,

\[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k) \le f(x) + C(x, k)\]

Now, we want to show that \(x_{k'} \ge x_{k}\). Equivalently, we want to show that all \(x \lt x_k\) cannot be optimal for \(i = k'\). Notice that if we can show the following:

\[x \lt x_k \implies f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') \le f(x) + C(x, k')\]

… we immediately prove that all \(x \lt x_k\) cannot be optimal since \(j = x_k\) achieves a smaller sum than \(j = x\).

Now, our problem reduces to the following: Show that

\[x \lt x_k \implies f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') \le f(x) + C(x, k')\]

First, note that \(x \lt x_k \lt k \lt k'\). Therefore, by the Quadrangle Identity, we have:

\[C(x, k) + C(x_k, k') \le C(x_k, k) + C(x, k')\]

Equivalently,

\[C(x_k, k') - C(x, k') \le C(x_k, k) - C(x, k) \text{ (Eq. 1)}\]

Adding \(f(x_k) - f(x)\) to both sides of Equation 1,

\[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') - f(x) - C(x, k') \le f(x_k) + C(x_k, k) - f(x) - C(x, k) \text{ (Eq. 2)}\]

Also, we know that:

\[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k) \le f(x) + C(x, k)\]

Equivalently,

\[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k) - f(x) - C(x, k) \le 0 \text{ (Eq. 3)}\]

Using Equation 3, we can rewrite Equation 2 as follows:

\[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') - f(x) - C(x, k') \le f(x_k) + C(x_k, k) - f(x) - C(x, k) \le 0\] \[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') - f(x) - C(x, k') \le 0\] \[f(x_k) + C(x_k, k') \le f(x) + C(x, k')\]

Notice: This is exactly what we wanted to prove. Therefore, \(x \lt x_k\) cannot be optimal for \(i = k'\), which implies that \(x_{k'} \ge x_k\), as needed. \(\blacksquare\)

Conclusion

When working with recurrences, it is always helpful to note the special properties of the sequences and functions involved. These special properties may allow one to optimize certain DP transitions. In this article, we were introduced to one such special property: The Quadrangle Inequality. Furthermore, we learned how this property enforces monotonicity in the minima of the DP transition, which we can then exploit with Divide and Conquer to optimize \(O(n^2)\) DP to \(O(n \log n)\).

Acknowledgements

I would like to acknowledge the NOI.PH trainers for help in answering some of my queries regarding this topic.

entry.title: Some Questions to Ponder
entry.content:
Background

Someone asked me to compile some of the questions I had about CompProg and list what I learned from these questions.

I have listed some of these questions. If I have an answer for it, there should be a link to a page that discusses the answer.

Questions

  1. How do you compute the nth term of an order-k linear recurrence faster than \(O(k^3 \log n)\)?
  2. In general, what is the order of an element of \(GL(n, \mathbb{Z} / p \mathbb{Z})\)?
  3. How can you compute min-plus convolution in subquadratic time?
  4. How do you prove the correctness of Dijkstra’s algorithm?
